speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Scarsdale, New York, interviewing Michael Librach. It's November 2nd, 1995, and I am Terry Tolle. Michael, you just told us a story where you might have had a very different kind of outcome. The commandant said that you should not be punished for pushing a German soldier. I wondered if you had had a less merciful commandant, what would have happened to you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some kind of a punishment. Still, you know, I have to repeat that we were under the Wehrmacht, and Wehrmacht was very jealous. Wouldn't give their Jews to the SS, so that there was no possibility of sending me into the concentration camp. That was never the question. The punishment could have been deprivation of some kind of a favor that they consider or the probation of-- or solitary for a few days or something like that. They would be administrative.

speaker: interviewer
text: So despite all our general knowledge that many German soldiers were very anti-Semitic, in this situation, in this camp, Jews were allowed to live and were not subject to the abuse that they were in other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only that. I consider we were the best-hidden Jews of Europe, because we were in their hands. We were protected by Geneva Convention, and everybody survived. 100% survival. So that-- you know, I don't think it would have happened if the war lasted longer that we would have come out with our lives. I don't think so, because the whole idea of putting us together in one camp was to have us there, you know, in case they decide to exterminate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was definitely a step towards extermination, there's no doubt about-- why would they put us together? They were not that afraid of contaminating the other people or the other prisoners of war. So anyhow, this is what happened. And in this camp, as I said, we were reporting news to other forced labor people especially. And they caught two of our men. And here is-- the punishment was to take the whole camp and transfer us somewhere where we would be afraid of dying. And that was across-- almost practically across the street, a few meters between us and concentration camp of Mauthausen. Just one minute. They caught two of our men doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just having those papers on them that we were distributing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Transmitting information about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Transmitting information about the war and so on, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for these two men--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't kill them, they didn't punish them, except they punished the whole camp. They took the whole camp, and they transfer us where we could not have the liberty. We had a tremendous amount of freedom there. A tremendous amount of freedom. I was, you know, with the two horses, I was all over the place. And so were other prisoners of war. So that they had to cut off that freedom of movement. And they took us over there to intimidate us. Because we have seen a camp of Mauthausen, which was not a camp of the ovens, but it was a camp where they killed them with work and starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see this from the other camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see-- I've seen skeletons standing with a shovel in their hands and trying to-- trying to breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were traveling with your horses and wagon, you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we were-- no, no, no. In the next camp, in the camps from Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, next to Mauthausen. We have seen a column of-- of prisoners from the concentration camp going into an underground factory who were just moving. They were not live people. Completely dead people. It's, you know, it was-- it was absolutely horrid. Absolutely horrid to see these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you think would happen to you when you were shipped over to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought that probably they might-- they might do something with us. I don't know. We were not that secure as I am now saying that, you know, we are under the Wehrmacht, so they won't do anything to us. Look, we tried to scream, get Geneva Convention. We tried to say things that, you know, came off our tongues very easily. But they could have done anything they wanted with us. But it was the Wehrmacht, and it was 1945. It was February 1945. The war was lost. They knew the war was lost. And I have a story. At that point, that shows how lost they knew they were. One Sunday morning, a very cold day, an SS man comes into my camp. And we are in the barracks, because Sunday, we are not working. And he comes into our bunk to warm himself up, into our barrack. And he sits at the table, and I just came to the table, and I started talking to him. And I find out that this man, with all the black and the SS on his lapels and so on, he's a former actor of the Max Reinhardt troupe, a Jewish director, well-known theatrical figure. And that just, you know, like stopped my breath.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how come-- how come that a person like you is an SS man? And he says, we are being mobilized. They know they lost the war. And to cover for the volunteers, they're mobilizing us. I said, but you have family. Do you tell them what's going on in this camp? He says yes, I go on furloughs. And I tell my family. And what they say? They don't believe me. They say I'm lying. So you know, at that point, Germany knew that they lost the war. And the killing of a few more Jews was not that important to them. So this was the situation until almost the end of April we were there of 1945. What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out there, just road work and things like that under guard, so that we didn't have any contact with the population at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you lose a lot of weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there-- there-- there was hunger already, because we were strictly on the food that the Germans were giving us. So there we were hungry, but you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not in Mauthausen. We were still under the very old guards, you have to understand this. It was the end of the war. The young people were at the front. They didn't waste young people on guarding prisoners. They were old people. Some of them were very anti-Nazi and so on. It's what was left. You know, some kids and some very old men that were guarding us. That's very different. And they knew that they lost the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next in April of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened in April was that they gave us one day's food, and they pushed us down a road to get away because the American army was coming towards us. And we were walking for about two weeks. Stopping in some villages, doing some odd work for people to get some food. Our guards would contract us to work for some people. And until we came to a village that I have no idea, I have no name for it, I don't know where, and it's close to Ravensburg. We were close to the Danube River. And I heard that the guards are-- were planning to cross the-- the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which river?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danube, the Danube River. It was pretty wide at that point. And that would put us away from the Americans. And that afternoon, when I heard that, I'm standing on a ridge. There's a valley in front of me and another ridge in far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through the valley, there are some German soldiers running. That's maybe like May 1st, or something like that, '45. Without arms. They're running away. They're complete disarray. And they come to my-- to the place where I'm standing. And I see a lot of dust away in-- at the horizon. And I say, hey, what's that dust? He says, that's American tanks. And when will they be here? Oh, probably tomorrow morning, because they don't work at night. They work 9 to 5. That's what they believed. Look, there was some-- some reason to that belief. Because at that time, the bazookas were in the hands of children, German children. They were lying at the roads in ditches with bazookas and hitting the American tanks. So the American tanks didn't advance too much in the night because of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, hearing that and knowing that my guards decided that they will move across the river, I decided that I'm not going to stay with them. So I went into a barn, and dig a hole in a pile of hay, and hid there. And I hid there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you broke away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I broke away from them. They didn't cross, and they were still there the next day. And the next morning, very early in the morning, I hear a big noise on the road in front of that farm, and I come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have to know that by that time, I didn't have a uniform of any nation. I was in a combination of all the uniforms. And first time I hear, stop, who's there? It's an American who doesn't know what he's seeing, you know. I said, I am friend, friend. I didn't know too much English yet at that time. And then the Americans start jumping out of the tank. And the first one said he's Swedish, the other is Ukrainian, the third one is Irish, and so on. I say, where are the Americans? Oh, we are all Americans. And that was my first encounter with the melting pot of United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out right away that the guards were still there. And they took care of the guards, the American soldiers. And here we are. And they say, just walk back, just walk back. So we walk back. And here we come upon a farm, and there must be 1,000 prisoners of war. Americans are sitting there examining. Doctors, you know, asking if everybody-- everybody is well, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they call me, because I know seven words of English, so I should translate. What happened was also that some of my friends found a big barrel of wine. And everybody's drinking, and I'm drinking. And I come-- I come upon some of my friends in the kitchen of the farm. They are sitting there. And somebody comes in and says, listen, do you have something for me to carry around?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, yeah, take a look at-- and there is a child's rucksack. You know, and I take the rucksack and I throw out what was in it through the window, which was level to the ground, the window. And those pages are spread there. And I have still-- I have this big saber that somebody found. And I am throwing the rest of the sheets, I am throwing out of the window. And a little boy comes up and is crying over every single page. And as much as I hated the Germans at that point, it was this first child's crying that I have seen, and I couldn't take it. I started crying. I just want to tell you that coming out of-- at that point, the way I felt, I think I felt the same way later on when I wrote-- I wrote a poem, which I think just to show you my feelings at that time. 'Because a bloody wound of our world you have made. Our brothers you have killed, our friends forced to degrade, because our children were hungry for years. The sound of your gloating, the sound of your jeers. Because we just wanted to live when you came to our land and killed. Killed in damned glory of your Hitlerland. For the war that brought our world to madness, for flyers that died in flames, for people drowned inside of your boats for me. For feeding my-- my dreams with cruelty and sadness in killing. For hatred that ties my heart in knots, I curse you, Germans. For shame to humanity, for bastardly pride, for cruelty and bloody for blood thirst. For following like sheep, for making our mothers weep. Anything that's German, per secula seculorem, be cursed.' That was my feeling. What we could do with it, we thought we could do the same things that they did. That we could be as brutal as they can be. But we are not this type of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Came to the next village, and my two best friends went to look for some kind of a place where we could stay. I was still with the Americans trying to translate. And I asked, where are my friends? And somebody says, go in the baker's house and you'll find them. And I come in, and I kick the door. I am the victor, I am the man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these two old Jews are sitting there with little German children in their lap, because we cannot kill children. We are not this type of people. No matter how we feel about Germans, no matter what they did to our children, we could not do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found the humanity through the children that you encountered those first few days--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you would-- you might not have expected, given how angry you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: The humanity in our hearts. In our hearts, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you want to have revenge once you were free?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I still have revenge. I don't buy German things. I never went-- I was very careful not to step on German soil when I went to Poland. I didn't want to stop in Berlin. I still hate the German language. I am trying to forget it. I knew it very well. I don't know it very well anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, so this is my revenge. But to hurt a child, to hurt another human being by now? At that time, maybe a man, yes. But to hurt another human being by now, it's just not-- not our nature. So this is-- this is emotionally what happened. That right away, I was proven. I had the proof in my hand that, you know, oh, I can feel very strongly about it, but that's all we can-- we can do. We can feel strongly about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is very peculiar that today, when I am making this interview, the things that I have told you about the Germans that I have encountered during this time were other acts of kindness. There were several acts of kindness that I have encountered. And although I know how-- how terribly they have treated us and so on, this is what I told you today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your honesty and your openness. Could we talk a little bit about how you got used to freedom? Here you are wearing clothes from several different countries' uniforms. You're traveling with American soldiers or under their protection. How did you get clothes? How did you get food? Where did you go next? How did this all fall into place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I went back to Paris. My repatriation could not be to Lodz, Poland. I had nobody there. I had no one to-- that I could be-- that I could go to. My family was here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So coming into Paris, I came in with somebody who had family in Paris. And we were demobilized. And we found ourselves in Paris street at 2 o'clock in the morning, free. Here. Here, you're finished with war. Here's 60-something per day, whatever it was. It was a few pennies that they paid us, because we were soldiers of the French army. And here you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friend says, OK, my family's not here, but my brother-in-law is here in Paris. So I'm going to call him, and we'll go to his house. And I said no, you can't. It's 2 o'clock in the morning. Don't wake up people. After five years of prison camp, I was very-- I was trying to save the two hours of sleep of these people. But anyhow, we went there in the morning. We said hello to them. I didn't stay there. I found a friend of mine who opened his house and his closet full of clothes to me. And of course, I got in touch with my parents, and they sent me money. And I lived in Paris until February 1946. When I got myself-- I went to the Ministry of War, and I decided-- I told them my repatriation is not complete until I get together with my family. At that point, there was no question of-- the question was only there was no boats. There was no space, because the American soldiers were coming home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they finally found me a spot on a-- on a little French boat, a little bath tub. And I took three weeks from Le Havre to New York in February on the Atlantic, which is, you know, a special pleasure of special kind. But, yeah. Between May of '45 and February of '46 when you set sail, did you have any physical or emotional problems? Sort of delayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had physical problems. I had physical. I got very-- I got very sick. My nervous-- probably my nervous system. It came out to be a sciatica, which all of a sudden, I had an attack. And I had to be taken into a military hospital in Paris. And I spend there six weeks, and had to relearn how to walk, and so on. Also some of it might have been caused by a little overdoing things that I haven't done for five years, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Catching up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Catching up, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Playing. You returned to your play mode.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mostly playing. Having good times. Yeah, I didn't work those months. I didn't have to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was it like being in Paris right after the war? Did you ever encounter any anti-Semitism, any comments about what the war was all about? Was it a comfortable place to be, or did you sort of feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved Paris from before the war. But after the war, yes, there were signs on the walls. There were signs in the streets. And the main sign that I have seen was the [SPEAKING FRENCH]. Which means, the gold Palestine has sold the France to Jews. You know, there was-- there were signs. There were anti-Semitic signs and so on. Something that you'd never seen in France before. I never-- I never did. It was very, very upsetting. Very upsetting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were ready to change environment, go someplace else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was ready anyhow, because my family was here. So I knew that I'm coming here. That I'm not going to stay in France, but it was very upsetting. I had-- during the war, when I was in France during the war, the French cook gave us so-called godmothers, young ladies who-- you know, because we were from another country. We were alone in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had these two marraine de guerre, they were called near Paris. Very, very bourgeois. French bourgeoisie, daughters of a doctor. And I went to visit them after the war. It was very changed, because everything was changed so completely. And but otherwise, I had nobody in France, you know, so I-- Speaking of change, were you able to find out anything about friends or relatives who had remained in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of them. I-- I knew most of them were killed. Of course, unusually high percentage of my friends from my school years and so on, unusually high percent survived. Some of them were caught abroad when the war broke out. And some of them were hidden or whatever and survived. Some of them were in Lodz ghetto, which was the last to be liquidated. And-- and you know, even people who were sent to Auschwitz survived. Because it was only a question of a few months, two or three months, and they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a very unusually high percentage of my friends have survived. And family, no. Not family. All my uncles and-- they're the ones that were in Poland, the ones that were left. And no, they didn't survive. Nobody survived. Primarily, I lost my sister and my nephew. What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in Warsaw ghetto, and I have no idea what happened. But in Warsaw ghetto, you know, nobody survived really except a few people that left at the very end with the resistance movement. So you probably had interviewed people from Warsaw ghetto, so you know these things, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: On February 26th, 1946. It will be 50 years in a few months, in a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city did you arrive at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in New York. My parents and my sister and her husband lived in Rockville Centre, New York on Long Island. And a few days later, I left for Philadelphia, where my father started a textile factory, to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned English there, partly at work and partly from movies. I went to the movies every night. I didn't know anybody in Philadelphia, so I went to the movies every night. And all my friends were in New York, and I would come for every weekend to New York. I had a little apartment in New York. And that's about one year in Philadelphia, and then we had to close that factory. We didn't produce very well, so that was-- that was another thing that went by. And then I started working for another company until I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any adjustments getting used to American ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I even got very, very, very enthusiastic about baseball, because the Dodgers signed Jackie Robinson. So then I learned all about baseball, because you know, my liberal tendencies, you know, made me interested in baseball. And I was completely crazy about the Dodgers until they left. So this-- yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share your story about the war with anyone here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell the story to anybody who wants to listen to it. Occasionally, I will pick out-- something comes up in a conversation, I will tell them one story or another. I never had occasion of doing it in its entirety like here, we're doing it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you first arrived, was it something you wanted to forget, or is it something you wanted to discuss?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wanted to discuss. I don't want to forget. Too many things happened that should be known. And I don't feel that even those-- the bad things, and especially maybe the bad things, because they should be avoided in the future. If we don't talk about it, then nobody will know. It can happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people willing to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. I had some friends who went through much worse things than I did they couldn't-- who didn't talk about it. Yes, but I did. I consider it my duty, because I didn't go through the terrors that they have seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married December 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years after I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two children. I have Peter, who was born in 1956. And Katherine, born in 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got married, I went back to school. I completed NYU Business School of Business Administration. And then I sat for the CPA exam, which is another story also which doesn't belong here so much. But I became a CPA, and that's how I brought up my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I wanted, at this point, if we can do a little bit of reflection. Why are you telling your full story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why am I telling the full story now? Just not to be forgotten. It's extremely important that I tell my story. And people who lived very different lives through the war should tell their story. And all this should be brought together. And I am extremely grateful to Steven Spielberg for thinking of-- of doing that, because he's doing that for my grandchildren and my great grandchildren, who someday will be able to just punch it into a computer and hear the stories. And these stories would have been forgotten. Because right now, I cannot tell them-- my grandchildren aren't too interested. And I know from personal experience, I got interested in the old things after my father died, and I can't-- have no way of finding certain things about my-- my ancestors. They will be lucky. They will have the history here. It's the history of Jewish nation. It's a history that's comparable with the suffering in the Crusades, and the suffering in the Khmelntytsky Uprising, and all throughout our history there were Holocausts. That's not the first one. But this one, we are able to put it on tape and have it, and have it documented.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the Holocaust has affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a tremendous extent. My life would have been very different. I would stay in Poland, live a normal life of some. I would have married, and have children, and so on. My life has changed completely, besides the emotional. Actually, you know, in the actual way. I am living in a country that I love now, and where I have all the rights and privileges. I lived in a country where I was second-class citizen, and that's how I would have lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Jew, how did the Holocaust affect your sense of identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also tremendously. I have never felt Jewish until I was told that I'm something that should be exterminated. And then I went through our history, and I tried to find out why? What happening? And I still don't understand the hatred that people in Montana have towards Jews, because they don't see any Jews. How do they know? And whether it's religious, or whether it's-- well, whatever it is, it's the hatred that scares me, you know, nowadays. It's the hatred that is shown that scares us. And of course it affected me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very inspiring to hear the love and respect for humanity that you've just expressed. And as an actor and a theater director, I know you try to convey that in your other life as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your story.
